skill.name: grant-budget-justification
skill.description: Generate structured narrative budget justifications for NIH and NSF grant proposals, covering personnel, equipment, supplies, and travel costs.
skill.summary: Generate narrative budget justifications for academic grant proposals with structured templates and explicit assumptions.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 50 patterns (risk score 100/100), but all 14 high-severity and 31 medium-severity flags are false positives: Markdown backtick code formatting triggered shell execution rules, and text keywords like 'execution' triggered cryptographic algorithm rules. The skill is a legitimate academic writing tool with a single low-risk concern: the Python script writes output to a user-controlled file path without path traversal validation at scripts/main.py:157. No network access, no credential handling, no code injection, and no prompt injection attempts were found.

medium_findings.title: File Write to User-Controlled Path Without Validation
medium_findings.description: The script writes output to args.output (from --output CLI argument) without validating the path for directory traversal sequences. A user could pass --output ../../unintended-file to write outside the intended workspace. This is low severity because the script is designed for local, user-initiated execution and the path is explicitly provided by the operator.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives - Markdown Backtick Formatting
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 31 locations as shell backtick execution across SKILL.md and references/audit-reference.md. Every flagged instance is standard Markdown code formatting (inline backticks or fenced code blocks) used for documentation, not executable shell commands injected from user input. All 31 findings are false positives.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives - Keyword Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: Fourteen high-severity 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags and six 'system reconnaissance' flags were triggered by text keywords in documentation and code comments (words like 'execution', 'script', 'input', 'path'). None of these locations contain cryptographic operations or system reconnaissance activity. All 20 findings are false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated File Output Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The argparse --output parameter accepts arbitrary paths and writes directly via open(path, 'w') without checking for path traversal sequences or restricting the write location to a safe directory.

content.value_statement: Writing budget justifications for grant proposals is time-consuming and requires careful formatting for agency compliance. This skill generates structured narrative justifications for personnel, equipment, supplies, and travel costs with explicit assumptions and clear output boundaries.
actual_capabilities: Generate narrative budget justifications for grant proposals with personnel, equipment, supplies, and travel cost categories
Produce personnel cost breakdowns with role descriptions, effort levels, and salary calculations
Create equipment justifications with cost-benefit rationale and usage descriptions
Format supplies and travel cost sections following NIH and NSF agency conventions
Output structured text with explicit assumptions, risks, and unresolved items documented
Run as a standalone Python script accepting JSON input and producing text or markdown output
limitations: Cannot verify actual market prices for equipment or supplies against vendor catalogs
Cannot access institutional salary data, fringe benefit rates, or indirect cost policies
Requires structured JSON input files for non-demo use and does not accept free-text budget descriptions
Output quality depends on completeness and accuracy of the input data you provide

use_cases.title: Research Grant Applications
use_cases.description: Principal investigators preparing NIH R01 or NSF grant proposals can generate structured budget justifications for personnel, equipment, and supplies with agency-compliant formatting.

use_cases.title: Grant Writing Support
use_cases.description: Research administrators managing multiple faculty submissions can produce consistent budget narratives quickly using standardized templates and input formats.

use_cases.title: Grant Writing Training
use_cases.description: Graduate students and postdocs learning grant writing can study the structure and requirements of budget justifications through generated examples and the demo mode.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Demo
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the grant budget justification script in demo mode to show example output for all cost categories.
prompt_templates.scenario: Testing the tool with built-in sample data

prompt_templates.title: Personnel Budget
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a personnel budget justification: PI Dr. Rivera at 25% effort with $130,000 salary, Co-I Dr. Chen at 15% effort with $110,000 salary, and a Postdoc at 100% effort with $55,000 salary. Use NIH format.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building the personnel section of an R01 budget

prompt_templates.title: Complete Budget Package
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a full NIH grant budget justification covering: personnel (PI 25%, Postdoc 100%, Graduate Student 50%), equipment (confocal microscope $95,000, computing cluster $18,000), supplies (laboratory reagents $22,000, animal costs $8,000), and travel (two conferences at $2,000 each).
prompt_templates.scenario: Preparing a complete proposal budget for submission

prompt_templates.title: Custom Input Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the budget justification script with my personnel.json and equipment.json files. Validate all input fields first and report any missing required data before generating the output.
prompt_templates.scenario: Production workflow with prepared data files

output_examples.input: Generate an equipment justification for a mass spectrometer at $50,000
output_examples.output:
B. Equipment

Mass Spectrometer: $50,000.00
  Justification: This instrument is essential for completing Aim 2 proteomics analyses. No existing departmental equipment can provide the required mass resolution and sensitivity. The quoted price reflects a 15% institutional discount from the approved vendor.
  Usage: Dedicated to this project at 80% capacity, with remaining time available for core facility shared use.

Total Equipment Costs: $50,000.00

output_examples.input: Create personnel justification for Dr. Smith as PI at 25% effort with $120,000 salary
output_examples.output:
A. Personnel

Dr. Smith (PI)
  Effort: 25% (3 months)
  Role: Project oversight and direction
  Justification: Dr. Smith will lead all research activities, ensure regulatory compliance, supervise personnel, and coordinate with collaborators. The 25% effort reflects the scope of project management and scientific direction required across all three specific aims.
  Cost: $30,000.00

Total Personnel Costs: $30,000.00

best_practices: Provide complete input data with all required fields before running the script to avoid partial or incorrect output
Review all generated justifications against your institution's specific salary policies, fringe rates, and equipment purchasing rules
Run the demo mode first to verify the tool works correctly in your environment before processing custom input files
anti_patterns: Submitting generated justifications to a funding agency without reviewing each line for accuracy and institutional policy compliance
Using path traversal sequences or absolute paths outside the workspace in input and output file arguments
Expecting the tool to fill in missing salary data, institutional rates, or vendor quotes without providing that information in the input files

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It generates structured narrative budget justifications for grant proposals, covering personnel, equipment, supplies, and travel cost categories with agency-compliant formatting.

faq.question: Which funding agencies does it support?
faq.answer: The default templates follow NIH format. NSF formatting is available as a secondary option through the agency parameter. Other agency formats are not currently supported.

faq.question: What software do I need to install?
faq.answer: The skill requires Python 3.10 or later. No additional third-party packages are needed beyond the Python standard library.

faq.question: Can it connect to my institution's financial systems?
faq.answer: No. The skill works entirely with data you provide. It cannot access payroll systems, vendor databases, purchasing platforms, or institutional financial records.

faq.question: How do I provide my budget data?
faq.answer: Supply JSON files with personnel, equipment, supplies, and travel data arrays. Each item needs name, cost, and justification fields. Run with --demo to see the expected data format.

faq.question: What output formats does it produce?
faq.answer: The skill outputs plain text by default. Markdown format is also supported through the --format parameter. The DOCX option is listed but may require additional Python libraries not included in the standard library.
